mnemonics.title: Remember Basic Gates
mnemonics.content: AND = ALL MUST BE 1; OR = ANY 1 IS OK; XOR = DIFFERENT = 1; XNOR = SAME = 1; NOT = FLIP IT

mnemonics.title: De Morgan's Theorem
mnemonics.content: FLIP THE BAR AND CHANGE THE SIGN: A+B becomes A.B (flip bars, change OR to AND)

mnemonics.title: Truth Table Rows
mnemonics.content: 2 TO THE POWER OF N: 2^n rows for n inputs

mnemonics.title: NAND Universal Gate
mnemonics.content: NAND CAN BUILD ANYTHING: NAND gates can create AND, OR, NOT functions

acronyms.usage: Produces 1 when inputs differ, used in addition and comparison
Produces 1 when inputs are equal, called equivalence gate
Selects one input from many based on select lines
Routes one input to one of many outputs
Used to model and simulate digital circuits like VHDL and Verilog

visualTips.visualization: Picture a series circuit with two switches: current flows ONLY when BOTH switches are closed (1).
Picture a parallel circuit with two switches: current flows when EITHER switch is closed (1).
Think of a light controlled by two switches at different positions: light is ON when switches differ.
For n inputs, imagine a binary counting pattern: 000, 001, 010... up to 2^n-1.
Combinational = calculator (no memory); Sequential = computer (remembers previous calculations).

analogies.analogy: Like a team where ALL members must agree (1) for the team to succeed (1). If any member disagrees (0), the team fails (0).
Like having multiple doors to enter a building: if ANY door is open (1), you can enter (1).
Like a camera with negative film: what is bright (1) becomes dark (0), and vice versa.
A pure function in programming: output depends ONLY on current input parameters, no variable state retained.
Like reading a book: your current understanding depends on what you read before plus what you are reading now.
